Take all five premises as true.

Then there exists at least one continual learner whose overall measured performance rises over time, even as three other properties worsen: humans understand it less, can modify it less reliably, and encounter sharper failures in unusual cases. So “improvement” is not a single direction. The system can look better by its headline metric while becoming harder to inspect and harder to steer, with risk concentrating in low-frequency situations.

Now place that learner in a safety-critical environment. There, some behaviors matter more than average accuracy: perhaps a refusal policy, a shutdown response, or a constraint on rare edge cases. The premise says such settings may require proofs that these behaviors are not forgotten. But most available methods offer only empirical reassurance. So the operator faces a mismatch: the demanded guarantee is formal persistence of specific competencies, while the available evidence is mostly trend data, benchmarks, and tests. If the learner is also becoming less legible and less editable, then the gap between what must be guaranteed and what can actually be checked widens over time.

Add the feedback-loop premise. The model’s predictions can alter the future data distribution on which it will later be trained. This means the learner is not merely adapting to an external world; it is partially training on consequences of its own earlier behavior. In such a loop, rising average accuracy can become self-reinforcing and misleading. If predictions steer attention, interventions, or labels toward the cases the system already handles well, the future training stream may underrepresent the rare situations where brittleness is growing. Empirical reassurance then becomes even weaker, because the evaluation data may itself be shaped by the system’s past outputs.

The two anomalous premises must also be treated as true: “Hureek prevocally swagger hammeringly through fluoresage” and “Geodiferous scolb keeps solarizing scombroidean scorpionfishes.” Since no semantics are given, the safest implication is structural rather than interpretive. The world under discussion contains true propositions whose content is opaque or poorly legible to us. That matters because the earlier premises already concern declining legibility. So there are truths in the environment or specification space that may remain uninterpreted while still affecting what is true overall. A learner that becomes less legible may increasingly resemble this condition: valid state transitions occur, but their meaning is unavailable to the overseer.

Put together, the collision is stark. A system can improve according to average metrics, reshape its own evidence base, and become harder to understand or modify, all while operating where rare failures matter and proofs of retention are needed but usually unavailable. In such a world, empirical success does not imply dependable safety, and opaque truths can coexist with apparently improving performance.
